params.description: 3×5 dot array displayed centrally, neutral emphasis.

beats.text: Let's figure out whether those two expressions really do always give the same product. Watch how I test this idea.

elements.description: 3×5 dot array displayed centrally, neutral emphasis.

params.description: 3 horizontal bands highlighted sequentially to emphasize rows.

beats.text: I see the rows. 3 rows of 5. That's 3 times 5. And 3 times 5 is 15.

elements.description: 3×5 dot array with row emphasis applied, 3 horizontal bands highlighted.

params.description: Row emphasis clears.
5 vertical bands highlighted sequentially to emphasize columns.

beats.text: Now the columns. 5 columns of 3. That's 5 times 3. And 5 times 3 is 15. Same product! Same array, same dots. I didn't add anything or take anything away. Just read it a different way.

elements.description: 3×5 dot array with column emphasis applied, 5 vertical bands highlighted.

params.description: Equation 3 × 5 = 15 displayed below array.
Equation 5 × 3 = 15 displayed below array alongside first equation.

elements.description: 3×5 dot array with column emphasis applied.
Equation 5 × 3 = 15 displayed below array alongside first equation.

params.description: 5×4 dot array appears in reading mode.
Row emphasis applied — rows highlighted going across.
Equation builder appears below array. 3 slots: factor × factor = product. Tile palette: 3, 4, 5, 6, 9, 10, 16, 20, 24, 25.

beats.text: Here's a new array. I highlighted the rows — see them going across? How many rows, and how many in each? Build the expression and find the product.
Build the equation for the rows.
5 times 4 equals 20. 5 rows of 4.

elements.description: 5×4 dot array with row emphasis applied.

params.description: Row emphasis clears. Column emphasis applied — columns highlighted going down.
Previous equation builder removed.
New equation builder appears for columns. Same 3-slot template and tile palette.

beats.text: Now I switched to columns. Build the column expression and find the product.
Build the equation for the columns.
4 columns of 5. 4 times 5 equals 20. Same product again.

elements.description: 5×4 dot array with column emphasis applied.

params.description: 7 × 3 dot array appears with row emphasis applied.
Equation builder appears in equation mode. Three blank slots for factors and product.

beats.text: Another array. Rows are highlighted. Build the row expression and find the product.
Build the equation for the rows.
7 times 3 equals 21.

elements.description: 7 × 3 dot array with row emphasis applied.

params.description: Column emphasis applied to array.
Row equation builder removed.
New equation builder appears for columns.

beats.text: Now columns. Build it.
Build the equation for the columns.
3 times 7 equals 21. Same product — again.

elements.description: 7 × 3 dot array with column emphasis applied.

beats.text: That's three arrays now. Every time — two expressions, same product. 15 and 15. 20 and 20. 21 and 21.

params.description: Static equation: 3 × 5 = 15
Static equation: 5 × 3 = 15
Static equation: 5 × 4 = 20
Static equation: 4 × 5 = 20
Static equation: 7 × 3 = 21
Static equation: 3 × 7 = 21

beats.text: Same product every time. But here's what we need to figure out: is this a coincidence, or does this ALWAYS happen? And if it always happens... why?
That's three arrays now. Every time there were two expressions, same product. 15 and 15. 20 and 20. 21 and 21. Why do you think both expressions give the same product?
Why do both expressions give the same product?
options: Same dots — you just read them a different way
The numbers are small enough to match
Rows and columns always have the same number
It only works for some arrays

condition.selected: Same dots — you just read them a different way

validator.description: Student selected correct answer: same dots read differently

beats.text: No dots changed. You just looked at the same dots a different way. Same dots has to mean same total. That's why the products match.
But can we prove it even more clearly? Watch this.

params.description: 3×6 dot array appears in neutral emphasis. 3 rows of 6 dots each.
Expression displayed: 3 × 6 = 18.

beats.text: Here's an array showing 3 rows of 6.

params.description: System briefly highlights rows.

beats.text: 3 times 6 equals 18. Now watch what happens when I turn this array.

elements.description: 3×6 dot array in neutral emphasis after row highlight.

params.description: Array physically rotates 90° clockwise. The 3×6 array becomes a 6×3 array. Smooth rotation animation lasting approximately 2 seconds.
Array is now 6 rows of 3 dots each.
Expression updates to: 6 × 3 = 18.

beats.text: I turned it. Look, what WERE rows are now columns. What WERE columns are now rows.

params.description: System highlights rows of the new orientation.

beats.text: Now it's 6 rows of 3. 6 times 3 equals 18. Same product.

elements.description: 6×3 dot array after rotation and row highlight. 6 rows of 3 dots each.

beats.text: And here's the important part, did any dots disappear when I turned it? Did new dots show up?
No. Every single dot is still there. I just turned the array. Same dots, same total. That's why the product can't change.

elements.description: 6×3 dot array. 6 rows of 3 dots each.

params.description: 5×3 dot array appears in reading mode.
System highlights rows sequentially to show 5 rows of 3.

beats.text: 5 rows of 3. That's 5 × 3, and 5 times 3 is 15. I'm going to rotate this array. Before I do, let's make a prediction. What expression will describe the rows AFTER I turn it?
After rotation, what will the row expression be?

params.description: Array rotates 90 degrees, becoming 3×5.
Array is now 3 rows of 5 dots.
System highlights rows to confirm 3 rows of 5.

beats.text: 3 times 5. The 5 rows of 3 turned into 3 rows of 5. And it has the same total of 15 still. You predicted it.

elements.description: 5×3 dot array in reading mode. Rows highlighted. Multiple choice tool active.

params.description: 8 × 5 dot array appears. 8 rows of 5 dots each. Rows highlighted.

beats.text: 8 rows of 5. 8 times 5 equals 40. If I turn this array, what will the new row expression be?
After rotation, what will the row expression be?

validator.description: Student selected 5 × 8, correct, understands rotation swaps factors

beats.text: 5 times 8. Rotation swaps the row and column counts.

elements.description: 8 × 5 dot array in reading mode. Rows highlighted. Student answered row expression prediction.

beats.text: And will the product change?
After rotation, will the product be the same or different?

validator.description: Student selected Same, correct, internalized that rotation preserves product

params.description: Array rotates. Now 5 rows of 8 dots. Expression updates to 5 × 8 = 40.

beats.text: 8 times 5. Still 40. Same dots, just turned.

elements.description: 5 × 8 dot array after rotation. 5 rows of 8 dots. Expression displayed: 5 × 8 = 40.

params.description: 3×6 array appears. 3 rows, 6 items per row, 18 dots total.

beats.text: Two different ways to prove the same thing. Highlighting: you read the same array two ways. Rotation: you turn the array and rows become columns. Either way, same dots, same product.

params.description: Rows highlight sequentially. 3 rows, 6 items each. 3 × 6 = 18.
Columns highlight sequentially. 6 columns, 3 items each. 6 × 3 = 18.
Array rotates 90 degrees. 3×6 becomes 6×3. Same 18 dots.
Array now shows 6 rows, 3 items per row. Same 18 dots.

beats.text: This doesn't just work for these arrays. It works for EVERY array. Any array you can build. The two expressions will always give the same product. Always.
Why do both expressions always give the same product?
options: Arrays are always the same size
Multiplication always gives the same answer

beats.text: Same dots. Whether you read them by rows or columns, whether you turn the array, the dots don't change. That's why the product can't change.

elements.description: 6×3 array displayed. 6 rows, 3 items per row, 18 dots total.

params.description: First equivalence: 3 × 5 = 15
Turn-around fact: 5 × 3 = 15
Second equivalence: 3 × 6 = 18
Turn-around fact: 6 × 3 = 18
Third equivalence: 5 × 8 = 40
Turn-around fact: 8 × 5 = 40

beats.text: You proved something important. No matter which array you check, the two expressions always give the same product. This shows us that you can multiply the factors in any order and always get the same answer. These are turn-around facts.
Mathematicians have a name for this. They call it the commutative property of multiplication. But the idea is simple, you can change the order of the factors and get the same answer.
And here's why this is more than just interesting. It's a strategy, a plan for solving problems. If you know one fact, you already know its turn-around fact without doing any extra work.

Watch how the strategy works. If I know 4 times 6 is 24, do I need to compute 6 times 4? No, I can multiply in any order. Switch the factors. 6 times 4 is also 24. I didn't compute anything. I used what I already knew.
Your turn. You know 7 times 8 is 56. What's 8 times 7? Use the strategy.

validator.description: Student selected 56, correct, turn-around facts applied

beats.text: 56. You didn't need to compute, you used the turn-around facts. You can multiply the factors in any order and get the same product.

params.description: 3×8 dot array appears as the reference. 3 rows of 8 dots. 24 dots total.
Four dot arrays displayed as answer options. A) 4×6 | B) 8×3 | C) 6×4 | D) 2×12. All have 24 dots.

beats.text: Here's an array: 3 rows of 8. That's 24 dots. Now look at these four arrays. They ALL have 24 dots. But which one is the SAME array as 3 × 8, just rotated?
Which array is the same as 3 × 8, just rotated?

validator.description: Student selected B) 8 × 3, correct. Rotation swaps 3 rows of 8 into 8 rows of 3.

params.description: Option B highlights to confirm selection.

beats.text: 8 rows of 3. That's 3 rows of 8, just turned. Same dots, same product. Now look at the others: 4 × 6, 6 × 4, 2 × 12. They all have 24 dots too, but they're different shapes. You can't rotate 3 rows of 8 and get 4 rows of 6. Same product doesn't mean same array.

elements.description: 3×8 dot array displayed as reference. 3 rows of 8 dots. 24 dots total.
Four dot arrays displayed as answer options. A) 4×6 | B) 8×3 | C) 6×4 | D) 2×12. All have 24 dots. Multiple choice tool active.

params.description: Static equation displayed: 9 × 4 = 36.
Equation builder appears below with three blank slots and tile palette showing numbers 3, 4, 6, 9, 13, 36, 49, 63.

beats.text: Given that 9 times 4 is 36, what turn-around fact do you know?
If 9 × 4 = 36, what turn-around fact do you know?

params.description: Placed tiles confirm: 4 × 9 = 36.

beats.text: 4 times 9 equals 36. You can multiply in any order and get the same product.

elements.description: Equation builder with three blank slots. Tile palette available. place_tile tool active.

beats.text: You proved it: when you change the order of the factors, the product stays the same. You used highlighting, rotation, and the turn-around strategy. Let's see what you know.
